scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines is a 4th-century Athenian orator discussing the laws of Athens regarding manumission in the city's theater.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech Against Ctesiphon was delivered in 330 BCE, referencing contemporary Athenian legal prohibitions.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim refers to crowns proclaimed in the city theater and dedicated to Athena, the patron goddess of Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines cites this as a standing law of the Athenian state during his 330 BCE trial.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The passage describes the standard procedure of the Athenian Ekklesia (Assembly) in the 4th century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The verbatim explicitly anchors the event to the spring of 340 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Voting on ambassadors to foreign states like Eretria and Oreus is a core function of the Athenian Assembly's sovereign power.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The specific motion described regarding Eretria occurred in 340 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines explicitly identifies the government of Oreus as a democracy where everything is done by popular vote.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The democratic actions of Oreus cited by Aeschines occurred during the diplomatic maneuvering of 341-340 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns the dedication of land to Apollo and the Amphictyonic oath following the First Sacred War.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This refers to the conclusion of the First Sacred War and the subsequent dedication of the Cirrhaean land around 590 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text names the Athenian Archon and citizens represented by their specific Athenian demes (Anaphlystus, Anagyrus).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The verbatim explicitly dates the archonship of Theophrastus to 340/339 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes the specific voting hierarchy and representative roles within the Pan-Hellenic Amphictyonic Council.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines describes these procedures in the context of his own service as a Pylagoras in 339 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The proclamation specifically commands all men of Delphi to perform labor on sacred grounds.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This emergency labor call occurred during the Amphictyonic crisis of 339 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule defines the composition of the 'oikoumene' or broader assembly of the Amphictyonic league.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This specific assembly was called by Cottyphus during the events leading to the Fourth Sacred War in 339 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines is invoking the Periclean citizenship law of Athens which required both parents to be citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines uses this standing Athenian legal requirement to attack Demosthenes' status during the trial in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text refers to a law passed by 'fellow citizens' governing ferrymen traveling to the Athenian territory of Salamis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This is cited as a contemporary Athenian law in force at the time of the speech in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Providing armor and maintenance to war orphans was a well-known public institution in Classical Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines describes this as a customary practice (though one he claims has been corrupted) in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'proedria' (honorary seating) in the theater was a specific civic honor granted by the Athenian state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The practice is cited during Aeschines' address to the Athenian jury in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Qualifications for a 'friend of the people' (populist leader) are framed within the context of Athenian democratic laws and norms.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines defines these ideal/legal requirements for Athenian leadership in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion from the Agora for cowardice is a specific penal provision of Athenian law attributed to Solon.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines argues this law remains in effect and should be applied to Demosthenes in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule refers to the legal restriction on who can be crowned and honored by the Athenian people.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines cites this as part of the current legal fabric of Athens during his prosecution in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Participation in the 'sacred rites of the people' refers to the state religion and civic cults of Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The exclusion of 'cowards' from rites was a standing Athenian legal penalty in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The orchestra and the shrine of Dionysus are specific physical and sacred spaces in the city of Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines is arguing against Ctesiphon's decree during the judicial review in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text refers to the Athenian Senate (Boule) and the historical event at Phyle involving the Athenian democrats.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule describes the specific review process for honors following the restoration of democracy in 403 BCE.
